title: The U.S. Navy Is Sweeping for Mines in the Strait of Hormuz — But It Could Take Months
summary:
The Strait of Hormuz is one of the most crucial waterways on the planet — about 20% of the world's oil flows through it every day. But right now, it's a high-stakes guessing game. President Donald Trump announced that the U.S. Navy is clearing Iranian mines from the strait, but experts say the operation could take months and may not fully restore confidence.

Minesweeping is not like in the movies. These aren't spiky floating balls. According to Emma Salisbury, a scholar at the Foreign Policy Research Institute, the mines are likely sitting on the seabed or moored just below the surface. They can be triggered by a ship's engine noise or the change in water pressure. And the biggest challenge? No one knows for sure if mines are even there. Iran has only mentioned the "likelihood" of mines in prewar routes. But as Salisbury put it, "You don't even have to have laid mines — you just have to make people believe that you've laid mines."

The psychological impact is huge. Even if the U.S. clears the strait, all Iran has to do is say, "Well, actually, you haven't found them all yet," and shipping companies and insurers may refuse to send vessels through. That could keep oil prices high and disrupt the global economy.

Pentagon officials told lawmakers in a classified briefing that it would likely take six months to clear the mines. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth did not deny the estimate but said the military would not speculate on a timeline. Trump has ordered the Navy to attack any boat laying mines and said he's "tripling up" the effort. But there is no indication the U.S. is using its most visible mine-clearing warships; instead, divers and small explosive ordnance disposal teams are likely doing the work.

Iran's mine stockpiles are estimated in the low thousands, mostly older Soviet models, with some newer ones from China or made domestically. Iran also has small submarines that can lay mines and are hard to detect. Steven Wills, a retired Navy lieutenant commander, compared minehunting to "walking through your yard pulling individual weeds" and minesweeping to "mowing the grass." Both are slow, painstaking processes.

Scott Savitz of the RAND Corp. noted that some areas from World War I and II still haven't been cleared because it's so resource-intensive. So even after months of work, the strait may not be 100% safe — but it might be safe enough. The question is whether the world will believe it.
why_it_matters: This story matters because disruptions in the Strait of Hormuz could send oil prices skyrocketing, affecting the cost of gasoline, heating, and shipping for people around the world. It also shows how modern conflict uses uncertainty as a weapon, where the fear of mines can be as powerful as actual mines.

keywords.explanation: The process of removing explosive mines from an area, usually underwater.
Fixed in place, like a mine anchored to the seabed.
The effect on people's minds and behavior, such as fear or uncertainty.
A secret meeting where officials share information that is not public.
Large collections of weapons or supplies kept for future use.
Requiring a lot of time, money, or equipment to accomplish.

options: No one knows for sure if mines are there
The mines are hidden on land
questions.correct_answer: No one knows for sure if mines are there

options: World War I and II mines still uncleared
Searching for a needle in a haystack
questions.correct_answer: World War I and II mines still uncleared

questions.question: Even after clearing, what could keep oil prices high?
options: Shipping companies refusing to sail through
questions.correct_answer: Shipping companies refusing to sail through

background_read: The Strait of Hormuz is a narrow waterway between Iran and Oman that connects the Persian Gulf to the Gulf of Oman. About 20% of the world's oil passes through it daily, making it a strategic chokepoint.
Mine warfare has been used for centuries; even today, some mines from World War I and II remain in the Baltic Sea and other areas because clearing them is so slow and dangerous.
Iran has one of the largest mine stockpiles in the Middle East, including older Soviet models and newer Chinese or Iranian-made mines. It also operates small submarines that can lay mines discreetly.

The U.S. Navy's mine-clearing capabilities include specialized ships and explosive ordnance disposal (EOD) teams, but in this operation they are likely using divers and small boats rather than large minehunters.
Article_Structure: LEAD: Strait of Hormuz – vital waterway, U.S. clearing mines
MINESWEEPING REALITY: Not like movies – mines can be seabed or moored, triggered by noise/pressure
└─ UNCERTAINTY: No one knows if mines are actually there – Iran only mentioned 'likelihood'
PSYCHOLOGICAL IMPACT: Fear alone can stop shipping and keep oil prices high
└─ QUOTE: Salisbury: 'You just have to make people believe'
TIMELINE: Pentagon estimates 6 months, Defense Secretary doesn't deny
└─ METHOD: Divers and small teams, not visible warships
IRAN'S CAPABILITIES: Thousands of mines, submarines, harder to detect
└─ COMPARISON: Minehunting = pulling weeds; minesweeping = mowing grass
HISTORICAL CONTEXT: Some WWI/II mines still uncleared – resource-intensive
OPEN QUESTION: Will the world believe the strait is safe enough?

perspectives.description: The U.S. Navy’s effort to clear mines is a necessary move to protect global trade and stabilize oil markets. Success could reassure shipping companies and prevent an economic crisis.

The operation highlights the risks of military escalation with Iran. Clearing mines is slow and expensive, and the psychological impact may persist for months, harming the global economy even if no mines actually exist.

Mine warfare is an asymmetric tactic — cheap for Iran to threaten, costly for the U.S. to counter. The situation illustrates how perception and deterrence shape military strategy, with the strait’s safety depending as much on belief as on physical clearance.

If the clearance succeeds and confidence is restored, it might set a precedent for international mine-clearing standards. However, if Iran continues to seed uncertainty, the world may need diplomatic solutions or alternative oil routes to reduce dependence on the Strait.
